 1.9         Duty to Inform Director of Bank Supervision in Financial Distress
             The Bank remains solvent and has not encountered any threats to its financial standing. A formal procedure is in place to ensure
             the Director of Bank Supervision of the CBSL is alerted before any decision is made regarding a potential insolvency event or a
             suspension of payments to depositors and other creditors.

 1.10        Maintenance of Statutory Capital Levels
             The Board of Directors actively evaluates the Bank’s capital position against regulatory benchmarks and internal risk thresholds.
             The Bank complied with all minimum capital adequacy standards during the year.
             The Management presents the capital position to the Board on a monthly basis vis a vis the Budget.
             Comprehensive Basel III Pillar 3 disclosures, as required under Banking Act Directions No. 01 of 2016, are set out in Annex 2 on
             pages 509 to 520.

 1.11        Publish Annual Corporate Governance Report
             Annual Corporate Governance Report of the Bank is set out on pages 216 to 232 of this Annual Report.

 2           Board Composition and Diversity
             The Board is composed of 11 Directors, including 09 Independent, INEDs, ensuring a high degree of autonomy and dedicated
             alignment with shareholder interests. The Board’s diverse makeup spanning various disciplines, genders, and professional
             backgrounds ensures a robust blend of experience and insight.
             Each Director contributes a wealth of expertise across diverse professional fields, fostering a culture of constructive challenge
             to Corporate Management and enriching deliberations on complex, dynamic issues.
             The Board further exemplifies the Bank’s commitment to diversity by integrating specialised knowledge across multiple
             disciplines, including:
                Finance & Banking: International Capital Markets, Risk Management, Banking and Accountancy.
                Technical & Scientific: IT, Science, and Renewable Energy.
                Industry & Professional: Agriculture, Chemicals, Law, Health, and Insurance.
             For further information refer "Composition of the Board” as at December 31, 2025 on page 223.

 2.1         Procedure for appointment of Directors
 (a)         Formal and transparent procedure
             The Board has established the BNGC with Terms of Reference that align with the Code of Best Practice on Corporate Governance.
             In collaboration with the BNGC, the Board has implemented a robust succession plan to ensure the seamless and orderly
             transition of Board appointments.

 (b)         Restrictions on Concurrent Directorships in Banking
             All Board members are compliant with the prohibition of concurrent roles at other domestic banks.
             There was no objection from the regulator on Ms. Lee’s dual directorships at DBS Group Holdings Ltd. and DBS Bank Ltd.
             (Singapore).

 (c)         Restriction for Directors representing shareholders
             Directors representing shareholders, who have acquired share capital carrying voting rights in contravention of Section
             12(1C) of the Banking Act No. 30 of 1988 (as amended), shall abstain from participating and voting in appointing independent
             directors.
             Bank does not have Director’s representing shareholders.

 2.2         Number of Directors
 (a)         The Bank’s Articles of Association mandate a Board size of seven (7) to thirteen (13) Directors. As at year-end 2025, the Board
             was comprised of eleven (11) members, within the prescribed statutory limits commensurate with the size, scale, diversity and
             complexity of the operation of the Bank.

 (b)         Board Gender Diversity
             The Board comprises three (03) female directors out of total board members of eleven directors as at December 31, 2025.
478     Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC     Annual Report 2025                          Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes



 Section       Principle, compliance, and implementation                                                                                            Complied


 2.3           No. of Executive Directors
               The Bank meets its regulatory obligations through a Board composed of two Executive Directors. The executive leadership is
               structured with one Director serving as the Managing Director/Chief Executive Officer, while the other serves as the
               Chief Operating Officer.

 2.4           Eligibility of Non-Executive Directors (NEDs)
               NEDs are experienced professionals with proven track records and the specialised expertise necessary to provide independent
               judgment on matters of strategy, performance, and resource management and contribute towards sustainability of the Bank.
               To facilitate appointments, the Board of Directors nominates eminent professionals or academics from diverse disciplines for
               consideration. These profiles are then reviewed by the BNGC, which evaluates each candidate's suitability before making
               formal recommendations to the Board.

 2.5           Independent Directors
 (a)           Composition of Independent Non-Executive Directors (INEDs)
               The Board has nine (09) INEDs which is well above the regulatory requirement to have at least half of the
               total number of Directors.

 (b)           Criteria for Determining the INED
 (i) to (ix)   All NEDs have successfully met the independence criteria prescribed by the regulator. The Board conducts an annual evaluation
               of each NED’s independence to ensure ongoing compliance with these regulatory standards.

 (c)           Identify INED and disclose categories of Directors in corporate communications
               The Bank expressly identifies INEDs in all corporate communications where Board members are listed. For a detailed breakdown
               of the Board’s composition categorised by Executive, Non-Executive, and Independence status are given on page 223.

 2.6           Procedures for Nominating Alternate Directors to Represent Independent Board Members
               There are no Alternate Directors on the Board of the Bank.

 2.7           Quorum and Composition Requirements for Board Meetings
               The Board strictly adheres to all quorum requirements and notably, the majority of its members are NEDs.

 3             Criteria to assess suitability of Directors
 3.1           Criteria to assess fitness and propriety of Directors
               The Bank maintains full compliance with Sections 42 of the Banking Act No. 30 of 1988 (as amended) regarding all Board
               appointments. Further, the Bank ensures the fitness and propriety of each Director by obtaining prior approval from
               the Director of Bank Supervision of the CBSL before any appointment is finalised.

 3.2           Additional Requirements for Suitability of Directors
 (a)           Age of Director should not exceed 70
               There are no Directors who are over 70 years of age.

 (b)           Tenure Limits for Non-Executive Directors
               No NED has served for more than nine years. Details of tenures of Director's are provided in “Composition of the Board, Board
               Committees and attendance at meetings ” on page 223.

 (c)           Directors should not be Directors of more than 20 companies/entities/institutions inclusive of subsidiaries or associate
               companies of the Bank.
               No Director holds directorships in excess of 20 companies/entities/institutions inclusive of subsidiaries or the associate
               of the Bank. Details of directorships are provided in “Board of Directors and Profiles” on pages 32 to 39.

 (d)           Director commitment and time allocation
               All Directors have demonstrated a strong commitment to their roles, dedicating sufficient time to fulfill the responsibilities
               entrusted to them by the Board.

 3.3           Cooling Off period for appointments of Bank Directors and CEOs
               No appointments for Director or CEO positions are approved, if they do not satisfy the regulatory requirement of 6 months
               cooling period.
Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes                           Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC   Annual Report 2025    479


 Section     Principle, compliance, and implementation                                                                                             Complied


 4           Delegation of Functions
 4.1         Division of Responsibilities
             Responsibilities within the control environment are clearly assigned. The Board oversees all delegation arrangements, ensuring
             they are executed in compliance with regulatory standards to ensure greater balance of power and authority.

 4.2         Board oversight and reserved authorities
             The Board utilises a formal schedule of reserved matters to ensure that all key strategic decisions are subject to their direct
             control and oversight.

 4.3         Limitations on Delegation of Authority
             Delegation to Board, Executive Directors and other KMP is structured in a manner which does not hinder the Board’s capacity for
             independent judgment and strategic oversight.

 4.4         Review of Delegation Process
             The Board reviews and approves the Bank’s delegation arrangements periodically to ensure they align with operational
             requirements while enabling the Board to fulfill its fiduciary duties effectively. Accordingly, the Board conducts a thorough
             review of the arrangements specified in Section 4.2 and 4.3 to maintain their oversight.

 5           The Chairman and CEO
 5.1         Separation of Roles of Chairman and CEO
             A clear distinction is maintained between the roles of Chairman and CEO. This separation of duties ensures a balance of power
             and authority, such that no single individual possesses unfettered decision-making control.

 5.2         Suitability of the Chairman
 (a)-(c)     Chairman should be a Non-Executive Independent Director or, if not independent, designation of an Independent                               Not
             Director as the Senior Director.                                                                                                      applicable
             Chairman is an INED.

 5.2         Responsibilities of the Chairman
 (a)-(i)     The Board of Directors is committed to maintaining the highest standards of Corporate Governance. Central to this
             commitment is the role of the Chairman, whose primary responsibility is to lead the Board and ensure its overall
             effectiveness. The Chairman has fulfilled the following duties in accordance with the Governance Framework.
             Board Leadership - The Chairman provided strategic leadership, ensuring the Board discharged its responsibilities effectively
             and maintained a clear distinction between Board oversight and executive management. Chairman ensured timely discussion
             of all key relevant issues.
             Agenda & Information - All meeting agendas were personally approved by the Chairman, incorporating input from fellow
             directors. The Chairman ensured that comprehensive briefing papers were distributed in a timely manner to allow for
             informed decision making.
             Board Dynamics - A culture of openness was fostered, where both Executive and Non-Executive Directors contributed
             actively. Ensure the Board receive adequate information in a timely manner. The Chairman specifically facilitated the
             involvement of NEDs to ensure independent perspectives were integrated into Board deliberations.
             Dissent & Debate - The Chairman encouraged critical and constructive debate. It is confirmed that dissenting views were
             freely expressed and recorded within the decision-making process without prejudice.
             Independence - In line with governance protocols, the Chairman remained independent of the day-to-day operations and
             did not engage in the direct supervision of KMP or other executive duties.
             Shareholder Relations - The Chairman ensured robust communication channels with shareholders remained open,
             ensuring their views were accurately represented and discussed at the Board level.

 5.4         Conduct of CEO
 (a)         The CEO serves as the apex executive responsible for the Bank's daily operations.
             Under the authority delegated by the Board of Directors, the CEO maintains primary accountability for the Bank’s ongoing
             management and operational performance.

 (b)         The CEO is not an employee or director of any other licensed bank or external entity.


 (c)         Positions held by the CEO within the Group are limited to Non-Executive directorships in subsidiaries and the effective discharge
             of duties as the CEO is not affected.
480     Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC    Annual Report 2025                          Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes



 Section     Principle, compliance, and implementation                                                                                             Complied


 5.5         Suitability of CEO
             In accordance with the Section 44A of the Banking Act No. 30 of 1988 (as amended), the Board of Directors maintains a rigorous
             process to ensure the suitability of the CEO in terms of authority, stature, knowledge, competencies etc., given the scale,
             diversity, complexity of the operation of the Bank.

 6           Board appointed Committees
             The Board has established ten Committees to oversee the Bank’s operations and five (5) are mandated by regulatory
             requirements, while the remaining five (5) were appointed to address specific strategic and business needs. Refer the
             “Governance Structure” on page 221.

 6.1         Requirements for the Board Committees
 (a)         In accordance with our internal governance protocols, each committee reports directly to the Board.


 (b)         The Board has clearly defined the scope of authority for each Committee. Further, serve in an advisory capacity, examining
             complex issues and providing strategic recommendations to the Board.

 (c)         Every Committee operates under a Board-approved Terms of Reference, which are reviewed periodically to ensure alignment
             with regulatory requirements.

 (d)         Each Committee has appointed a Secretary responsible for organising meetings and maintaining comprehensive minutes.
             Under the supervision of the respective Committee Chairpersons, minutes and records are maintained in sufficient detail to
             reflect the deliberations and decisions made. All committee minutes are submitted to the Board for formal review.

 (e)         The quorum for each Committee meeting is set at a minimum of half the Committee members. During the reporting period, all
             meetings held met or exceeded this quorum requirement.

 (f )        The Board presented a report on the performance of each Committee, at the AGM. The Chairpersons of all Committees will be
             present at the AGM to address any specific queries directed to them. The performance of Committee are detailed under Board
             Committee Reports on pages 233 to 257.

 6.2         Board Audit Committee (BAC)
 (a)         The BAC is chaired by an INED. He does not chair the Board or any of the other Committees. He possesses professional
             qualifications and experience in accountancy and auditing with memberships of recognised professional accounting
             bodies; his profile is detailed on page 38.

 (b)         The Committee is comprised entirely of INEDs. Members possess skills & expertise in finance, accounts, auditing commensurate
             with scale, diversity & complexity of operations.

 (c)         The BAC and BIRMC has been structured in a manner that a majority of the Members of one Committee do not serve on the
             other.

 (d)         In accordance with the Terms of Reference, the BAC has made the following recommendations:
             (i) The appointment of the External Auditor for audit services to be provided in compliance with the relevant statutes;
             (ii) The implementation of the Central Bank Guidelines issued to External Auditors from time to time;
             (iii) The application of the relevant Accounting Standards; and
             (iv) The service period, audit fee and any resignation or dismissal of the External Auditor.
             (v) The BAC ensures that the service period of the External Auditor shall not exceed six (6) years, and that Engagement Partner
                 is not re-engaged for the audit before the expiry of three (3) years from the date of the completion of the previous term.

 (e)         Review and monitor External Auditor’s independence, integrity and objectivity and the effectiveness of the audit processes
             The Board has adopted a policy of rotation of Auditors, once in every six (6) years, in keeping with the requirements
             of the Directions and best practice.
Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes                          Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC   Annual Report 2025   481


 Section     Principle, compliance, and implementation                                                                                            Complied


 (f)         Provision of Non-Audit Services by the External Auditor
             The Bank adheres to a comprehensive, Board approved policy governing the engagement of the External Auditor for non-audit
             services. To safeguard objectivity and professional skepticism, the following procedural safeguards are implemented prior to
             any such engagement.
             (i) The Bank evaluates whether the specific skills, technical expertise, and institutional experience of the audit firm render it
                 the most suitable provider for the proposed non-audit service.
             (ii) The Bank requires formal representations and relevant disclosures from the External Auditor to verify their internal
                  independence.
             (iii) In accordance with the Bank’s policy, all non-audit engagements require mandatory pre-approval from the BAC
                   followed by final approval from the Board of Directors.
             (iv) Assess whether the nature of the non-audit services, the fee levels individually and in aggregate relative to the
                  audit firm, do not pose any threat to the objectivity and/or independence of the external auditor.
             (v) If the Management determines that the provision of a non-audit service is likely to impair or be perceived to impair the
                 auditor’s independence or objectivity, the assignment is prohibited.

 (g)         Determines scope of audit
             The Committee discussed the Audit Plan's scope for year 2025, with the External Auditors to ensure it encompasses:
             (i) A thorough assessment of compliance with Corporate Governance Directions and internal financial reporting controls.
             (ii) The preparation of financial statements in accordance with relevant accounting principles and disclosure requirements.
             (iii) Effective collaboration between audit firms involved in the Group’s multi-firm audit engagements.

 (h)         Report on Internal Control and Financial Reporting
             The Committee conducted a comprehensive review of the Bank’s accounting policies, systems, and internal control framework.
             The Committee is satisfied that the Bank maintains an efficient internal control environment ensuring transparency & integrity
             in the financial reporting process. Assist the BOD in discharging the responsibility under the “Directors’ statement on internal
             control over financial reporting and risk management” on pages 266 and 267.

 (i)         Financial Reporting Oversight
             The BAC is responsible for monitoring the integrity of the Bank’s financial disclosures. This includes a thorough review of
             quarterly and annual financial statements, disclosure reports, and the Annual Report, with a specific focus on the soundness of
             significant financial reporting judgements. In its evaluation, the BAC focus on the following key areas.
             (i) Assessing the estimates and assumptions that significantly impact reported figures.
             (ii) Reviewing any transitions or updates in accounting policies and practices.
             (iii) Evaluating material adjustments arising from the external audit process.
             (iv) Validating the appropriateness of the going concern assumption in the preparation of accounts.
             (v) Ensuring strict adherence to applicable Accounting Standards and statutory requirements.
             The BAC provides its formal recommendations to the Board of Directors on a quarterly basis to ensure transparency and
             informed decision-making.

 (j)         Discussions with External Auditor on interim and final audits
             The BAC discusses any issues, concerns, or reservations arising from interim and final audits in consultation with the External
             Auditor. During the period, the Committee held two (2) meetings with the External Auditor, independent of the Bank’s executive
             management.

 (k)         Review of management letter and Bank’s response
             The BAC has reviewed the External Auditor’s Management Letter along with the corresponding responses provided
             by Management.
482    Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC   Annual Report 2025                             Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes



 Section    Principle, compliance, and implementation                                                                                               Complied


 (l)        Oversight of Internal Audit Function
            The BAC has overseen the internal audit function to ensure its integrity and effectiveness.
            (i) The Committee reviewed and satisfied itself regarding the adequacy of the scope, functions, and resources of the
                Internal Audit Department and ensured that the department operates with the necessary authority to discharge
                its duties comprehensively across all Bank operations.
            (ii) The annual internal audit programme was reviewed and ensured that management implemented appropriate corrective
                 actions on recommendations of the Internal Audit Department.
            (iii) Conducted formal appraisals of the performance of the Chief Internal Auditor ie Deputy General Manager - Management
                  Audit (DGM-MA), and senior internal audit staff to maintain high standards of operational excellence.
            (iv) All appointments, terminations, and engagements of outsourced service providers related to the internal audit function
                 were reviewed and recommended by the Committee.
            (v) In instances where resignation of senior internal audit staff (including DGM-MA) or outsourced service providers, the
                Committee was duly apprised and provided the departing parties with the opportunity to submit and discuss their
                reasons for resignation, ensuring transparency and continuity.
            (vi) The Committee confirms that the internal audit function remains strictly independent of the activities it audits.
                 The function was performed with full impartiality, proficiency, and due care, in alignment with polices on internal auditing.

 (m)        Internal investigations
            The Committee reviewed significant findings resulting from internal investigations, along with responses and corrective
            actions proposed by management.

 (n)        Attendees at BAC Meetings
            The MD/CEO, COO, CFO, CRO, DGM-MA, Compliance Officer or other KMP and representatives from the External Auditors attend
            Committee meetings by invitation.
            To ensure independent oversight, the Committee gathered twice during the financial year with the External Auditors in the
            absence of Executive Directors. For further details regarding these deliberations and the Committee’s activities, refer to the
            "Report of the BAC" on pages 233 to 237.

 (o)        Authority, Resources, and Information Access
            The BAC is vested with following powers under its Terms of Reference ;
            (i) Explicit authority to investigate any matter falling within its scope.
            (ii) Provision of all necessary resources required to discharge its duties effectively.
            (iii) Unrestricted access to all relevant corporate information and records.
            (iv) Authority to engage independent professional advisors and invite subject matter experts to meetings as deemed
                 necessary.
            Refer the “Report of the BAC” on pages 233 to 237.

 (p)        Regular Meetings
            The BAC convened nine (9) times during the year, with eight (8) scheduled quarterly meetings and one (1) ad hoc sessions to
            address emerging matters. Members received timely notice and materials for all agenda items. Formal minutes, documenting
            the Committee’s deliberations and resolutions, were maintained by the Secretary to ensure an audit trail of its duties and
            responsibilities.

 (q)        Maintain minutes of Meetings
            DGM-MA serves as the Secretary for the BAC and maintains minutes of the Committee Meetings.

 (r)        Whistleblowing Policy
            The Bank maintains a Whistleblowing Policy, formally reviewed and approved by both the BAC and the Board of Directors. The
            Board’s commitment to fostering a culture of transparency and reporting non-compliance or unethical conduct is mandated in
            the Board Charter.
            The Bank has established mechanisms to ensure that concerns raised by employees, specifically regarding financial reporting,
            internal controls, or other improprieties undergo fair, independent investigation and prompt remedial action.

 (s)        Relationship with External Auditor
            The BAC is the key representative body for overseeing the Bank’s relations with the External Auditor and meets the External
            Auditor on a regular basis to discharge this function.

 6.3         Board Human Resources and Remuneration Committee (BHRRC)
 (a)-(b)     The Committee is chaired by an INED who does not serve as the Chairman of the Board, ensuring a clear segregation of duties
             and objective oversight. The Committee is further constituted with a majority of INEDs, fostering an environment of impartial
             decision-making.

 (c)         The CEO attended Committee meetings only upon invitation to provide necessary insights and was strictly excluded from
             discussions regarding their own performance or remuneration.

 (d)         The Committee determined and reviewed the Remuneration Policy covering salaries, allowances, and both financial and non-
             financial benefits for EDs and NEDs, and other KMP. This includes frameworks for termination and retirement benefits.

 (e)-(f)     The Committee established clear goals and targets for the EDs and NEDs and other KMP at the beginning of the period.
             Performance was periodically evaluated against these targets to determine performance-based incentives and revisions to
             remuneration packages.

 (g)         The Bank maintains adherence to transparency regarding terminal benefits. Prior written approval from Shareholders is
             obtained for any special payments or benefits made to Directors, the CEO, or KMP upon retirement or termination.

 (h)         The Committee ensured by coordinating with the BIRMC that all compensation structures remain within the Bank’s defined
             risk appetite and do not encourage excessive risk taking.

 (i)         A Claw-back Policy for performance based payments to the CEO and KMP in instances of inter alia fraud, misappropriation,                 Effective
             to the extent of the financial loss. This policy is integrated into employment Contracts, ensuring procedural propriety and              January
             transparency.                                                                                                                            01, 2026

 6.4         Board Nominations and Governance Committee (BNGC)
 (a)         Composition & Independence
             The Committee is chaired by an Independent Director who does not serve as the Chair of the Board. It maintains a majority
             of Independent Directors. The CEO attended meetings by invitation as and when required and was not present when matters
             related to him were discussed.

 (b)         Appointment of Directors, CEO and KMP
             The Committee has developed and implemented a procedure to appoint new Directors, CEO and the KMP of the Bank.
             The Committee has also developed a proactive process for planning and assessment of candidates for the succession of
             Executive and Non-Executive Directors, CEO and other KMP appointments within the Bank and its Subsidiaries.
             The Committee also oversee appointment and composition of the Sharia Supervisory Board (SSB or Sharia Board) of the
             Bank’s Islamic Banking Unit (IBU).
             Refer the “Report of the BNGC” on pages 241 to 243.

 (c)         Ensure of Fit and Proper persons
             The Committee obtains annual declarations from the Board of Directors and KMP, to verify their continued adherence to the
             'fit and proper' criteria mandated by Section 8.2 of the Directions and applicable statutes upon their initial appointment or
             following any significant change in designation to ensure ongoing regulatory compliance.

 (d)         Appointment of CRO, DGM – MA and Compliance Officer
             In recommending the CRO and DGM – MA, the Committee consulted BIRMC. Similarly, the views of the BAC were obtained for
             the appointment of the DGM – MA.

 (e)         Re-election of Directors
             The Committee provides recommendations for the re-election of existing Directors, evaluating their individual performance
             and substantive contributions toward the Board’s collective mandates. These assessments are conducted in strict accordance
             with the provisions set forth in the Articles of Association. For further details, please refer to the section 08 of the ‘Statement of
             Compliance’ on page 259.

 (f)         Independence Monitoring
             On a quarterly basis, the Committee reviewed the independence status of Independent Non-Executive Directors. No conflicts
             of interest were identified that impede independence, and all status updates were communicated to the Director of Bank
             Supervision.
484    Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC   Annual Report 2025                           Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes



 Section    Principle, compliance, and implementation                                                                                              Complied


 (g)        Eligibility criteria for appointments to KMP including CEO
            The Committee defines the eligibility requirements for appointing or promoting individuals to key managerial positions,
            including the CEO. These criteria cover essential qualifications, professional experience, and core attributes, all of which are
            developed in accordance with relevant statutes and regulatory guidelines.

 (h)        Succession plan and new expertise to the Board of Directors
            The Committee has established a succession plan for Directors. When the Board or its Committees identify a need for specific
            new expertise, they will notify the BNGC, which is responsible for taking the necessary action to address these requirements.

 (i)        Succession plan for CEO and KMPs.
            A proper succession plan is in place for the CEO and KMP. This includes identifying internal and external talent for short
            to long term needs, implementing grooming programs to bridge skill gaps, and performing an annual review of the plan’s
            effectiveness.

 (j)        Continuous professional development
            Directors remained updated on evolving laws, macroeconomic policies, technological shifts, and emerging market trends
            through regular briefings and board papers.

 (k)        Training and development of the Directors
            The Committee identified specific training needs for individual directors and recommended targeted capacity-building and
            professional development programs throughout the year.

 (l)        Monitoring of Board structure
            A comprehensive review of the structure, size, qualifications and composition of the Board and its Committees was conducted
            to ensure the Board remains "fit for purpose" in discharging its duties.

 (m)        Review of governance framework
            The Committee reviewed and updated the Bank’s overall Corporate Governance framework and Policies to align with
            industry best practices and the latest regulatory directives.

 6.5        Board Integrated Risk Management Committee (BIRMC)
 (a)        Independence of Chair
            The Committee is chaired by an Independent Non-Executive Director who does not serve as the Chair of the Board or
            any other Board Committee.

 (b)        Composition of the Committee
            The Committee is comprised of five (5) INEDs that possess collective sound experience in risk management practices within
            the banking and financial services sector. Refer the “Report of the BIRMC” on pages 238 to 240.

 (c)        Separation from other Committees
            The majority of the Committee members are not members of the BAC and vice-versa, ensuring clear functional separation.
            Refer the “Report of the BIRMC” on pages 238 to 240.

 (d)        Attendance of KMP
            The Managing Director/CEO and the Chief Risk Officer (CRO) shall attend the meetings of the Committee as Ex-Officio invitees.
            In addition, Chief Operating Officer, Chief Financial Officer (Secretary to the Committee), Chief Information Officer, Compliance
            Officer, and personnel supervising the risk categories of credit, market, liquidity, operational, technology and strategic risk will
            be permanent invitees to meetings of the Committee.

 (e)        Close collaboration with KMPs
            The Committee maintained close working relationships with KMP, making delegated decisions on behalf of the Board
            within its Terms of Reference.

 (f)        Independent Risk Management Function
            An independent risk management function has been established, covering all the activities lead by the integrated risk
            management department of the Bank.
Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes                          Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC   Annual Report 2025   485


 Section     Principle, compliance, and implementation                                                                                            Complied


 (g)         Risk assessment
             The Committee has approved the policies on Credit Risk Management, Market Risk Management and Operational Risk
             Management, Information Security Risk Management and Strategic Risk Management which provide a framework for
             management and assessment of risks. Further, Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process is being reviewed by the
             Committee annually. Assessing Credit, Market, Liquidity, Operational, Information Security, and Strategic Risks against
             the Board-approved Risk Appetite Statement. Risk profiles of the group companies were monitored through periodic
             review of KRIs and comprehensive annual risk review. Refer the "Report of the BIRMC" on pages 238 to 240.

 (h)         Reporting on Risk Appetite
             Regular reports were submitted to the Board regarding the Bank’s actual exposures against the Board-approved
             Risk Appetite Statement. A comprehensive Risk Assessment Report, detailing the Committee's deliberations, concurrences,
             and specific directions, is submitted to the Board within a week of each meeting.

 (i)         Oversight of functions of the CRO
             The Committee monitored the functioning of the CRO and received a regular report regarding the risk profile and any
             breaches of established limits.

 (j)         Strategy Oversight
             The Committee reviewed strategies implemented by the CEO and management for capital and liquidity management,
             and all other risks relevant to the Bank ensuring those remained consistent with the Bank's risk appetite.
             Refer the “Report of the BIRMC” on pages 238 to 240.

 (k)         Review of management level Committees on risk
             The Committee shall review the adequacy and effectiveness of all management level Committees such as Credit Policy
             Committee, Asset and Liability Management Committee (ALCO), Executive Integrated Risk Management Committee (EIRMC)
             etc., to assess their adequacy and effectiveness in addressing specific risks and managing them within the quantitative and
             qualitative risk limits specified by the Board of Directors. These limits are set out in the Risk Appetite Statement of the Bank
             and are reviewed by the Board on a regular basis.

 (l)         Corrective action to mitigate risks exceeding prudential levels
             Actual exposure levels under each risk category are monitored against the tolerance levels when preparation of “Risk Profile
             Dashboard” of the Bank, which is circulated among members of the BIRMC monthly and discussed in detail at quarterly
             meetings.
             The Committee takes prompt corrective action to mitigate the effects of specific risks in case such risks are at levels beyond the
             prudent levels decided by the Committee on the basis of the Bank’s policies and regulatory and supervisory requirements.

 (m)         Meeting Frequency
             The Committee meets quarterly and schedules additional meetings when required. The agenda covers matters assessing all
             aspects of risk management including updated business continuity plans. The Committee met five (5) times during 2025.

 (n)         Actions against officers responsible for failure to identify specific risks or implement corrective action
             The Committee refers such matters, if any, to the Human Resources Department for necessary action with observations and
             suggestions.

 (o)         Risk Assessment Report to the Board
             A comprehensive report of the meeting is submitted to the Board after each Committee meeting, by the Secretary of the
             Committee for their information, views, concurrence or specific directions.

 (p)         Compliance function
             A compliance function has been established to assess the Bank’s compliance with laws, regulations, regulatory guidelines,
             internal controls and approved policies on all areas of business operations. This function is headed by a dedicated Compliance
             Officer who reports to the BIRMC. The Compliance Officer submits a Positive Assurance Certificate on Compliance with
             mandatory banking and other statutory requirements on a quarterly basis to the BAC and BIRMC.

 (q)         Board Audit Committee Coordination
             An effective coordination mechanism was maintained with the BAC to exchange information on emerging risks for the Bank
             and ensure the integrity of the risk management framework.
486    Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC   Annual Report 2025                           Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes



 Section    Principle, compliance, and implementation                                                                                             Complied


 (r)        Remuneration Review
            The Committee examined employee incentive structures to ensure they appropriately reflect the Bank's risk, capital, liquidity,
            and earnings profile, without prejudice to the tasks of the BHRRC.

 6.6        Board Related Party Transactions Review Committee (BRPTRC)
 (a)        Committee Leadership
            The Committee is chaired by an Independent Non-Executive Director who does not serve as the Chair of the Board.

 (b)        Composition of the Committee
            The Committee comprised three (3) INEDs. The CEO and the KMP attended meetings only by invitation for specific agenda
            items.

 (c)        Policy and Process
            The Bank has established and maintained robust policies and processes for the identification, monitoring, and reporting
            of RPTs on an ongoing basis, in strict alignment with applicable laws. Refer the “Report of the BRPTRC” on pages 246 and 247.

 (d)        Transaction Review
            All RPTs, including those defined under the section 7.2 of the Direction, were reviewed by the Committee to ensure those
            transactions were conducted at arm’s length by the parties who are identified as Related Parties under section 7.1 of the
            Direction to mitigate any potential conflicts of interest.

 (e)        RPTs reporting to the Board
            The Committee submitted quarterly reports to the Board of Directors, providing comprehensive details on identified related
            parties, transaction volumes, and the resulting economic consequences.
            (Note 62 to the Financial Statements on “Related Party Disclosures” on pages 430 to 435).

 (f)        Conflict of Interest
            Directors refrain from participating at relevant sessions, in which lending to related entities are discussed to avoid any kind of
            influence and conflict of interest.

 (g)        Prohibited transactions
            The Bank’s Related Party Transactions Policy prohibits transactions, which would grant related parties more favorable
            treatment than that accorded to other customers. These include the following:
            (a) Granting of “total net accommodation” to related parties, exceeding a prescribed percentage of the Bank’s regulatory
                capital;
            (b) Charging of a lower rate of interest than the Bank’s best lending rate or paying more than the Bank’s deposit rate for a
                comparable transaction with an unrelated comparable counterparty;
            (c) Providing of preferential treatment, such as favorable terms, covering trade losses and/or waiving fees/commissions, that
                extend beyond the terms granted in the normal course of business undertaken with unrelated parties;
            (d) Providing services to or receiving services from a Related Party without an evaluation procedure;
            (e) Maintaining reporting lines and information flows that may lead to sharing potentially proprietary, confidential or
                otherwise sensitive information with related parties, except as required for the performance of legitimate duties and
                functions.

 7          Related Party Transactions
 7.1        Identification of Related Parties
            The Related Party Transactions Policy approved by the Board, define all Related Parties (Related Parties as specified in the
            Direction has been covered)

 7.2        Type of Related Party Transactions
            The Related Party Transactions Policy approved by the Board, covers the following transactions:
            (a) The grant of any type of accommodation, as defined in the Banking Act Directions on maximum amount of accommodation;
            (b) The creation of any liabilities of the Bank in the form of deposits, borrowings and investments;
            (c) The provision of any services of a financial or non-financial nature to the Bank or received from the Bank;
            (d) The creation or maintenance of reporting lines and information flows between the Bank and any related parties,
                which may lead to sharing of potentially proprietary, confidential or otherwise sensitive information that
                may give benefits to such related parties.
Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes                            Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC   Annual Report 2025   487


 Section     Principle, compliance, and implementation                                                                                              Complied


 7.3         Compliance with provisions of Section 47 of the Banking Act with regard to the accommodation to Related Parties
             The Bank has maintained strict adherence to the provisions of Section 47 of the Banking Act. No. 30 of 1988 (as amended).
             During the financial year, all financial accommodation granted to parties identified as RPTs were within the prescribed
             conditions, except for accommodation granted to CEO other KMP under the schemes applicable to employees.
             The Bank has adopted a robust framework to identify and monitor 'Related Parties' as strictly defined under
             section 7.1 of the Direction.

 7.4         Policy for granting accommodation to a Directors and/or Connected Parties
             Securities as approved by the CBSL to be provided within one year of appointment as a Director, o/a facilities obtained from the
             Bank in the name of the Director, close relation or connected entities.
             (not applicable to a director who at the time of the grant of the accommodation was an employee of the Bank and the
             accommodation was granted under a scheme applicable to all employees).
             Above is governed by the Board Approved Related Party Transaction Policy of the Bank.

 7.5         Policy for accommodation granted to Employees
             The Bank does not extend preferential treatment or special accommodation to its employees beyond established staff benefits.
             Per operational circulars, all staff members must refrain from granting favorable terms to colleagues, close relation, or any
             entity in which they or their Close Family Member hold a substantial interest. Maintaining impartial professional standards
             is a core requirement of the Bank’s internal governance.

 7.6         Writing-off of accommodation to Related Parties
             No such situation has arisen during the year

 8           Senior Management
 8.1         Board Oversight on Senior Management
 (a)-(f)
             The Board maintained diligent oversight of the Bank's day-to-day operations without interfering in executive functions.
             This was achieved by defining clear lines of authority, and responsibility holding regular performance reviews to monitor
             alignment with business strategy and adherence to policies.
             The Board engage with Senior Management as required to critically evaluate operational reports and independently assess the
             technical expertise and leadership competencies of the management team.

 8.2         Criteria to assess the Fitness and Propriety of Senior Management
             In terms of sections 42(2), and section 76H of the Banking Act No. 30 of 1988 (as amended), all members of the Senior
             Management of the Bank have been assessed and found to be ‘fit and proper’ persons to hold office.

 8.3         Responsibilities of Senior Management
             Following primary responsibilities were complied by the Senior Management of the Bank;
             (a) Upheld the highest ethical standards to foster a sound Corporate Governance framework.
             (b) Devoted ample time to their roles, ensuring subsidiary duties did not hinder Bank responsibilities.
             (c) Under Board direction, management successfully implemented all business strategies and risk management systems.
             (d) Respected and maintained the full independence of the Risk, Compliance, and Audit functions.
             (e) Regular training was undertaken to ensure competencies remain aligned with industry developments.
             (f ) Duties were effectively delegated to staff with continuous management oversight and accountability.
             (g) Promoting transparency and accountability was maintained across the Bank.
             (h) Prompt remedial and disciplinary actions were enforced following any identified policy or regulatory breaches.
             (i) Comprehensively updated the Board and Committees on performance, risk appetite, and material control matters.
             (j) Fulfilled its duty to report any material concerns regarding the fitness and propriety of officials.

            and presentation of the Financial Statements for external stakeholders adhere strictly to applicable accounting standards and

Annex 1.2: Compliance with Code of Best Practice on Corporate Governance
Compliance with the Code of Best Practice on Corporate Governance 2023 (the Code) issued by The Institute of Chartered
Accountants of Sri Lanka

 Code    Principle, compliance and implementation                                                                                              Complied
 ref.

 A.      Directors
 A.1     The Board
         The Board of Commercial Bank comprises eleven eminent professionals drawn from multiple fields and nine out of them are
         Non-Executive Directors (NEDs). They bring diverse perspectives and independent judgement to deliberate on matters set
         before the Board.
         Directors are elected by shareholders at the AGMs with the exception of the Managing Director (MD)/Chief Executive Officer
         (CEO) and the Chief Operating Officer (COO) who are appointed by the Board and remain as Executive Directors (EDs)
         until retirement, resignation or termination of such appointment. Casual vacancies are filled by the Board based on the
         recommendations of the Board Nominations and Governance Committee (BNGC) as provided for in the Articles of
         Association. The Board is assisted by the Company Secretary.

 A.1.1 Regular meetings
         The Board meets on a monthly basis and each Board Committee also has its own schedule of meetings as set out in the
         respective Committee reports. The regularity of Board meetings and the structure and process of submitting information
         have been agreed to and documented by the Board. Attendance at meetings is summarised in Table 78 on page 223.
         Information required to be reported to the Board under this Section is reported on a regular basis.

 A.1.2 Role and responsibilities of the Board
         The roles and responsibilities of the Board are set out in the Board Charter as summarised on page 222.

 A.1.3 Act in accordance with laws
         The Board has an approved working procedure in place to facilitate compliance with the relevant laws, CBSL Directions
         and guidelines and international best practice with regard to the operations of the Bank. This includes provision to obtain
         independent professional advice as and when necessary by any Director coordinated through the Company Secretary.

 A.1.4 Access to advice and services of Company Secretary
         All Directors are able to obtain the advice and services of the Company Secretary. The appointment and removal of the Company
         Secretary is a matter involving the whole Board under the advisement of the BNGC, as it is a Key Management Position.
         The Bank has obtained appropriate insurance coverage as recommended by the BNGC for the Board of Directors and the
         Members of the Corporate Management.
         Refer Section on “Role of the Company Secretary” on page 224 for further details on role of the Company Secretary.

 A.1.5 Independent judgement
         The Board comprises of senior professionals who are luminaries in their respective fields and use their independent
         judgement in discharging their duties and responsibilities on matters of strategy, performance, resource allocation, risk
         management, compliance, and standards of business conduct. The composition of the Board ensures that there is a sufficient
         balance of power and contribution by all Directors which minimises the tendency for one or a few members of the Board to
         dominate the Board processes or decision-making.

 A.1.6 Dedicate adequate time and effort to matters of the Board and the Company
         Board meetings and Board Committee meetings are scheduled well in advance and the relevant papers are circulated 07
         days prior to the meeting using electronic means to ensure that Directors have sufficient time to review the same and call
         for additional information or clarifications, if required. While there is provision to circulate papers closer to the meeting in
         exceptional circumstances, this is generally discouraged. Members of the Corporate Management Team and external experts
         make presentations to the Board on the business environment, regulatory changes, operations, and other developments on a
         regular basis to enhance the knowledge of the Board on matters relevant to the Bank’s operations.
         The NEDs dedicate approximately eighty-four days per annum for the affairs of the Bank and those Directors who are also on
         the BAC and the BIRMC dedicate a further ten days each for the affairs of the Bank.

 A.1.7 If necessary in the best interest of the Bank, one-third of the Directors can call for a resolution to be presented to the Board.

 A.1.8 Board induction and training
         Refer Section on “Induction and Training of Directors” on page 226.
Annex 1: Compliance with Corporate Governance Directions, Rules and Codes                          Commercial Bank of Ceylon PLC   Annual Report 2025   491


 Code     Principle, compliance and implementation                                                                                               Complied
 ref.

 A.2      Separating the business of the Board from the executive responsibilities for Management of the Company
          The positions of the Chairman and the CEO have been separated in line with best practice in order to maintain a balance of power
          and authority. The Chairman is an Independent Non-Executive Director whilst the CEO is an Executive Director appointed by the
          Board. The roles of the Chairman and the CEO are clearly justified and highlighted in the Annual Report and the Board Charter.

 A.3      Chairman’s role in preserving good Corporate Governance
          The Chairman provides leadership to the Board, preserving order and facilitating the effective discharge of duties of the Board
          and is responsible for ensuring the effective participation of all Directors and maintaining open lines of communication with
          KMP, acting as a sound Board on strategic and operational matters. The agenda for Board meetings is developed by the Chairman
          in consultation with the Directors, the CEO, and the Company Secretary, taking into consideration matters relating to strategy,
          performance, resource allocation, risk management, and compliance. Sufficiently detailed information on matters included in the
          agenda is provided to the Directors on time. Both Executive Directors and NEDs ensure the balance of power on the Board, for
          the benefit of the Bank, by effectively participating in decision making. All Directors have been made aware of their duties and
          responsibilities and the Board and Committee structures. All Directors are encouraged to seek information necessary to discuss
          matters on the agenda. Views expressed by Directors on issues under consideration are recorded in the minutes.

 A.4      Availability of financial acumen and knowledge to offer guidance on matters of finance
          The Chairman of the BAC who is a NED is a Fellow Member of CA Sri Lanka ensuring a sufficiency of financial acumen within the
          Board on matters of finance. Additionally, EDs and a NEDs are professionals with vast experience on matters of finance.

 A.5      Board balance
          The Chairman is an Independent Non-Executive Director. The Board comprises of nine NEDs and two EDs facilitating an
          appropriate balance within the Board. All NEDs are independent of management and free of business dealings that may
          interfere with the exercise of their unfettered and independent judgement. They submit annual declarations to this effect
          which are evaluated to ensure compliance with the criteria for determining independence in line with the requirements of the
          applicable regulations and this Code.
          There are no Alternate Directors or Senior Independent Directors.

 A.6      Provision of appropriate and timely information
          Board Members receive information regarding matters set before the Board a week prior to the meetings. The Chairman
          ensures that all Directors are properly briefed on same by requiring the presence of members of the Corporate Management
          when deemed necessary. Management also makes presentations on regular agenda items to the Board and its Committees.
          Additionally, the Directors have access to members of the Corporate Management to seek clarifications or additional
          information on matters presented to the Board. Directors who are unable to attend a meeting is updated on proceedings
          through formally documented minutes, which are also discussed at the next meeting to ensure follow-up and proper
          recording. Minutes of a meeting is ordinarily provided to Directors at least within two weeks after the meeting date.

 A.7      Appointments to the Board: Nomination Committee
          Refer Section on “Appointments and retirements/resignations of Directors” given on page 225 and Report of the BNGC
          on pages 241 to 243.

 A.8      All Directors should submit themselves for re-election at regular intervals
          Refer Section on “Re-election/election of Directors” on page 226 and “Report of the BNGC” on pages 241 to 243.
          In the event of resignation of a Director prior to completion of his/her appointed term, such resignation including reasons
          for decision shall be communicated in writing.

 A.9      Appraisal of Board and Board Committee performance
          Refer Section on “Board and Committee evaluations” on page 227.

 A.10     Annual Report to disclose specified information regarding Directors
          Information specified in the Code with regard to Directors is disclosed within this Annual Report as follows:
          Refer pages 32 to 39 for the Profiles including qualifications, expertise, material business interests and key appointments.
          Refer pages 271 and 272 for details on Directors’ Interest in contracts with the Bank.
          Refer page 223 and pages 233 to 257 for Membership of committees and attendance at Board meetings and Committee
          meetings.
          Refer Note 21 to the Financial Statements on page 351 for the details on Remuneration paid to Directors.
          Refer Note 62 to the Financial Statements on Related Party Disclosures on pages 430 to 435.

 A.11     Appraisal of the CEO
          Refer section on “Appraisal of the MD/CEO” on page 227.

 B.      Directors’ remuneration

 C.      Relations with shareholders
 C.1     Constructive use of the AGM and conduct of other General Meetings
         The AGM provides a forum for all shareholders to participate in decision-making matters reserved for the shareholders which
         typically include proposals to adopt the Annual Report and Accounts, election and re-election of Directors and Auditors and
         other matters requiring special resolutions as defined in the Articles of Association or the Companies Act No. 07 of 2007 (as
         amended). Separate resolutions are proposed for each material issue. The Chairman ensures the presence of the Chairmen of
         the BAC, BHRRC, BNGC and BRPTRC to respond to any questions that may be directed to them. Notice of the AGM is circulated
         together with the Annual Report and Accounts which includes information relating to any other resolutions that may be set
         before the shareholders at the AGM fifteen working days in advance. The Bank ensures that all valid proxy appointments
         received for the AGM are counted and properly recorded.
         A summary of the procedures governing voting at General Meetings is included under “Shareholder engagement and voting”
         section on page 230 of this Annual Report.
         Where a vote is required on a show of hands, the Bank will ensure that information required under the Code will be made
         available at the meeting and be published in the website within a month from the date of the AGM.

 C.2     Communication with shareholders
         The Policy on Relations with Shareholders and Investors sets out multiple channels of communication for engaging with
         shareholders. Channels include investor section of the website at http://www.combank.lk/investors, press releases and notices
         in English, Sinhala and Tamil newspapers and required disclosures to the CSE which are published on the CSE website. The
         Bank’s website provides information on risk management, debenture issues, economy and financial markets in addition to the
         financial information. The Interim Financial Statements are published in English, Sinhala and Tamil newspapers within stipulated
         deadlines. Every effort is made to ensure that the Annual Report provides a balanced review of the Bank’s performance.
         The principal forum for shareholders is the AGM, while matters can also be raised through the Company Secretary.
         The Company Secretary keeps the Board apprised of issues raised by the shareholders to ensure that they are addressed in
         an appropriate manner in keeping with the corporate values of the Bank. Matters raised in writing are responded to in writing
         by the Company Secretary.

 C.3     Disclosure of major and material transactions
         The Policy on Relations with Shareholders and Investors addresses the need to disclose major and material transactions to
         shareholders as required by the rules and regulations of the SEC and the CSE and the Bank has in place a defined process to
         comply with the requirements. There were no transactions which would materially alter the Bank’s or Group’s net assets nor
         any major related party transactions apart from those disclosed as follows:
            Refer page 230 for details on “Shareholder engagement and voting”.
            Refer Note 62 to the Financial Statements on Related Party Disclosures on pages 430 to 435.

 D.      Accountability and audit
 D.1     Present a balanced and understandable assessment of the Company’s financial position, performance, business model,
         governance, structure, risk management, internal controls, and challenges, opportunities and prospects
         All efforts are taken to ensure that the Annual Report presents a balanced review of the Bank’s financial position, performance,
         Business Model, Governance, Structure, Risk Management, Internal Controls, and Challenges, Opportunities and Prospects
         combining narrative and visual elements to facilitate readability and comprehension. Due care has been exercised to ensure
         that all statutory requirements are complied within the Annual Report and the issue of interim communications on financial
         performance which are reviewed by the BAC and recommended prior to publication. The following disclosures as required by
         the Code are included in this Report:

          In the unlikely event of the net assets of the Company falling below 50% of Shareholders’ Funds, the Board will summon an
          Extraordinary General Meeting (EGM) to notify the shareholders of the position and to explain the remedial action being taken.
          The Annual Report clearly explains how net assets have increased during the year in the Financial Review on pages 172 to 179.

 D.2      Process of risk management and a sound system of internal control to safeguard shareholders’
          investments and the Company’s assets
          The Board is responsible for determining the risk appetite for achieving the strategic objectives and formulates and implements
          appropriate processes for risk management and internal control systems to safeguard shareholder investments and assets
          of the Bank. The BIRMC assists the Board in discharge of its duties with regard to risk management and the BAC assists the
          Board in the discharge of its duties in relation to internal control which in turn is supported by the Inspection Department.
          Their responsibilities are summarised in the respective Committee Reports and have been formulated with reference to the
          requirements of the Code and the Direction on Corporate Governance and the Bank’s business needs. The BIRMC is supported
          by the Integrated Risk Management function of the Bank and a comprehensive report of how the Bank manages risk is given
          in the Section on “Risk Governance & Management” on pages 273 to 296 , the Report of the BIRMC on pages 238 and 240 and
          Statement of Directors’ Responsibility for Financial Reporting – Refer pages 264 and 265.

 D.3      Board Audit Committee (BAC)
          The BAC comprises of 4 independent NEDs and a summary of its responsibilities and activities are given in the Report of the BAC
          as appearing on page 233. It is supported by the Internal Audit function of the Bank reporting directly to the BAC.
          Refer Report of the BAC on pages 233 and 234 for the purpose, duties and responsibilities.
          The Board also obtains assurance from its External Auditors on the effectiveness of internal controls on financial reporting
          which is given on page 268.

 D.4      Board Integrated Risk Management Committee (BIRMC)
          The Committee assist the Board oversight of determining risk appetite, identification and classification of risks, reviewing risk
          management outcomes and mitigation actions, process to manage ESG risks etc. The Committee comprises of 5 INEDs.
          MD/CEO and CRO attend meetings on Ex-officio invitees. The CFO who is also the Secretary to the Committee attends all
          meetings. Chief Information Officer (CIO) and other heads of Strategic Business Units participate at the meetings by invitation.
          A summary of role, responsibilities and activities of the Committee are given in the Report of the BIRMC on pages 238 to 240.

 D.5      Board Related Party Transactions Review Committee (BRPTRC)
          The BRPTRC comprises of 3 Independent NEDs. The two EDs also attend the meetings by invitation. A summary of
          responsibilities and activities are given in the Report of the BRPTRC on pages 246 and 247. The Bank has a Board-approved
          Related Party Transactions Policy in place which addresses requirements under this section.

 D.6      Code of Ethics
          The Bank has an internally-developed Code of Business Conduct and Ethics which is applicable to Directors, other KMPs,
          and all other employees. The Bank also has a Board approved policy applicable to dealing in shares of the Bank which is
          fully compliant with the Listing Rules of the CSE.
          The Code of Conduct of the Bank is in compliance with the requirements of the Schedule N of the “Code of Business Conduct
          and Ethics” which encompasses conflict of interest, bribery and corruption, entertainment and gifts, accurate accounting and
          record-keeping, fair and transparent procurement practices, corporate opportunities, confidentiality, fair dealing, protection
          and proper use of Company assets including information assets, sexual harassment, discrimination and abuse, compliance with
          laws, rules and regulations (including insider trading laws) and encouraging the reporting of any illegal, fraudulent, or unethical
          behaviour. Further, the Bank has a well-established whistle-blower process in order to deal with complaints received from various
          stakeholders in relation to non-compliance with the Bank’s code of business conduct and ethics.
          Refer sections on “Code of Business Conduct & Ethics” and “Whistleblowing” on pages 228 and 229.

 D.7      Corporate Governance disclosures
          The Annual Corporate Governance Report from pages 216 to 232 comply with the disclosure requirements of the Code.

 E. & F. Institutional Investors and Other Investors Shareholder Voting
         The Bank has 22,695 ordinary voting shareholders as at end 2025, of which 840 are institutional shareholders. The Bank has
         regular dialogue with the large institutional shareholders and any concerns of these institutional shareholders expressed at
         the meetings are communicated to the Board as a whole. All shareholders are encouraged to exercise their voting powers at
         the AGM. The Bank facilitates the analysis of its securities by encouraging both foreign and local analysts covering the Bank
         with structured meetings where they are able to obtain information and explanations required for evaluating the current and
         future performance of the Bank, sector and country. Additionally, the investor relations section on the Bank’s website has key
         information required by shareholders and analysts. The Interactive Annual Report also has a tab where investors can provide
         feedback and request for specified information.
         All prospectuses include a clause which require all prospective investors in shares and debentures of the Bank to seek
         independent professional advice before investing.

 G.      Internet of things and cyber security
         Refer section on “Internet of things, cyber security and data privacy” on page 230.

 H.      Sustainability: ESG Risks and Opportunities
         The Board considers Sustainability/ESG-related risks and opportunities in the Bank’s business model, short and medium term
         planning and in its long-term strategy that could reasonably be expected to affect the Bank’s prospects.
         The Board and the KMP engage with and consider the views of its stakeholders to better understand and manage the Bank’s
         Sustainability/ESG-related risks and opportunities and have integrated sustainability/ESG related policies and practices into
         Bank’s strategy, business model, governance and risk management.
         The Annual Report contains information, which enables investors and other stakeholders to assess how ESG risks and
         opportunities are recognised, managed, measured and reported as described under following sections of the Annual Report.
            Stakeholders Engagement on pages 54 to 57 and Material Matters on pages 58 to 65.
            Business model for sustainable value creation on pages 66 to 69.
            Embedding sustainability into operations on pages 78 to 177.
            Risk Governance and Management on pages 273 to 296.
         This Annual Report has been prepared in accordance with International <IR> Framework, GRI Guidelines, “A Preparer’s
         Guide to Integrated Corporate Reporting”, “Handbook on Integrated Corporate Reporting” and “Guideline on Non-financial
         Reporting” published by CA Sri Lanka.

         should attend etc.

 9.7.1        The Bank shall take necessary steps to ensure that their Directors and the CEO are, at all times, fit and proper

 9.7.2        The Bank shall ensure that persons recommended by the Board Nominations and Governance Committee as
              Directors are fit and proper as required in terms of these Rules before such nominations are placed before the
              shareholders’ meeting or appointments are made.

 9.7.3        A Director or the CEO of the Bank shall not be considered ‘fit and proper’ if she or he does not possess the fit and
              proper assessment criteria specified under “Honesty, Integrity and Reputation”, “Competence and Capability”
              and “Financial Soundness” in Rule 9.7.3 (a), (b) and (c) respectively.

              them have continuously satisfied the Fit and Proper Assessment Criteria set out Rule 9.7.3 during the financial
              year concerned and satisfies the said criteria as at the date of such confirmation.

 Rule        Principle, compliance and implementation                                                                                       Complied

 9.8.1       The Board of Directors of the Bank, at a minimum, consist of five (05) Directors.
             The Board of Directors of the Bank consist of eleven (11) Directors.
             Profiles of the Board of Directors are given on pages 32 to 39.

 9.8.2       Minimum Number of Independent Directors
             (a) The Board of Directors of the Bank shall include at least two (2) Independent Directors or such number
                 equivalent to one third (1/3) of the total number of Directors of the Listed Entity at any given time,
                 whichever is higher.
                  The Board has nine (09) Independent Directors, which is well above the requirement.
                  Refer Table - 78 on “Composition of the Board, Board Committees and attendance at meetings” on Page 223.
             (b) Any change occurring to this ratio shall be rectified within ninety (90) days from the change.
                  There was no change to this ratio during the year.

 9.8.3       A Director shall not be considered independent if he/she does not fulfil the criteria defining under Rule 9.8.3 of
             the Listing Rules.
 9.8.4       All the Independent Directors of the Bank fulfil the criteria on independence as defined under Rule 9.8.3 of the
             Listing Rules.

 9.8.5       Process in place for the determination of ‘Independence’ of the Directors
             (a) Each Director to submit a signed and dated declaration annually of his/her independence or
                 non-independence.
             (b) Names of Directors determined to be ‘independent’.
                 Refer Table – 78 on “Composition of the Board, Board Committees and attendance at meetings” on page 223.
             (c) Immediate market announcement to be made if the Board determines that the independence of an
                 Independent Director has been impaired.

 9.9         Requirements to be complied in relation to “Alternate Directors”.                                                                Not
             There are no Alternate Directors on the Board of the Bank.                                                                    Applicable

 9.10.1      The Bank shall disclose its policy on the maximum number of directorships its Board Members shall be
             permitted to hold in the manner specified in Rule 9.5.1. In the event such number is exceeded by a Director(s),
             the Entity shall provide an explanation for such non-compliance in the manner specified in Rule 9.5.2 above.
             As per the Terms of Reference of the Board Nominations and Governance Committee, which is in compliance
             with the requirements of the Banking Act Direction No. 05 of 2024 and amendments thereto, maximum
             number of directorships the Board Members are permitted to hold is twenty (20).

 9.10.2      The Bank shall, upon the appointment of a new Director to its Board, make an immediate Market
             Announcement setting out the brief resume of such Director, his/her capacity of directorship and, Statement
             by the Entity indicating whether such appointment has been reviewed by the BNGC of the Bank.

 9.10.3      The Bank shall make an immediate Market Announcement regarding any changes to the composition of the Board
             Committees referred to in Rule 9.3 above containing, at minimum, the details of changes including the capacity of
             directorship with the effective date thereof.
